description: Cross-stack validation parity manifest. Each entry below is a payload that BOTH the Python encoder (offchain/src/aegis/auth_payload.py::encode_auth_coverage_payload) and the TypeScript encoder (frontend/src/wallet/aegis/auth_payload.ts::encodeAuthCoveragePayload) MUST reject before producing canonical CBOR. The `error_category` field labels the validation rule violated; tests in test_cross_stack_validation.{py,ts} assert each stack rejects with an error matching the listed pattern. Drift detection: if either stack ever loosens a rule, this manifest's CI gate catches it before the divergence ships. v3.1 (Δ36 / VR-006) adds the 5 length-check rules the original v2 manifest did not cover (oracle_nft length, pool_script_hash length, pool_nft length, signer_pkh consistency between AuthWitnessDatum.insured_vkey and the payload's insured_pkh field, and 64-byte signature length); these rules pin BOTH Python and TS encoders to the canonical hash widths so a 27-byte oracle_nft (typo) or a 65-byte signature (forgery via length-extension class) cannot ship past the off-chain pre-signing boundary.

base_payload.comment: A canonical, valid preprod payload used as a starting point. Each invalid vector below is `base_payload` with a single field mutated to violate exactly one rule. Coverage = 100 ADA so every vector except #6 satisfies the 5-ADA floor; not_before / not_after form a 7-day window so every vector except #9 satisfies the time-window invariant. The base also pins an `insured_vkey_hex` + `signature_hex` so the v3.1 rules #11 and #15 can mutate those fields independently of the canonical `AuthCoveragePayload` (the off-chain encoder enforces those rules on the surrounding sign_auth flow, not the bare payload encoder — the manifest still pins them as cross-stack contracts).

vectors.name: domain_tag_wrong_length
vectors.comment: 26-byte domain_tag (missing trailing `D`) — both encoders must reject.

vectors.name: network_magic_unknown
vectors.comment: 12345 is not a recognized Cardano network magic; both encoders must reject.

vectors.name: policy_validator_wrong_length
vectors.comment: 27-byte policy_validator (one short of the BLAKE2b-224 standard).

vectors.name: insured_pkh_wrong_length
vectors.comment: 27-byte insured_pkh — payout_address body still equals these 27 bytes plus the leading 0xc3 from the canonical 28-byte body, so the payout-binding check would actually pass (it is a length check that fires first).

vectors.name: payout_body_does_not_match_insured_pkh
vectors.rule: payout_address[1:] must equal insured_pkh (Δ9 — closes the rewire-payout attack)
vectors.comment: 29-byte CIP-19 enterprise address with the right testnet header (0x60) but a body of 0x99 bytes that diverges from insured_pkh — the attacker-rewires-payout attack. Δ9. The 0x99 body is exactly 28 bytes so the LENGTH check passes; the body-mismatch check is the one that fires.

vectors.name: max_coverage_below_5_ADA_floor
vectors.rule: max_coverage must be >= 5_000_000 lovelace (5 ADA, Δ14 / F-2)
vectors.comment: 1 lovelace coverage — relays refuse this; off-chain spam DoS via 1-lovelace policies is impossible.

vectors.name: max_coverage_exceeds_int63_cap
vectors.comment: 2^63 — exactly one over the cap that aligns Python with TypeScript. Cross-stack symmetry.

vectors.name: oracle_provider_out_of_range
vectors.rule: oracle_provider must be one of {0=Charli3, 1=Orcfax, 2=AegisSelf} (Δ5 / F-AUTH-5)
vectors.comment: Constr index 3 is undefined for the OracleProvider sum type.

vectors.name: not_after_not_after_not_before
vectors.rule: not_after must be > not_before (zero / inverted window rejected)
vectors.comment: not_after == not_before — degenerate window; on-chain validity-range check would pass at exactly the boundary, but the v2 spec requires strict > to bound the eligibility window (and a relay must have a non-zero observation window).

vectors.name: oracle_freshness_exceeds_24h_cap
vectors.rule: oracle_freshness must be <= 86_400_000 ms (24h sanity cap)
vectors.comment: 1 week (604_800_000 ms) — far over the 24-hour sanity cap; closes the wallet-UX deception surface where a malicious frontend could claim 'freshness = 5 minutes' but encode a multi-day value.

vectors.name: oracle_nft_wrong_length
vectors.rule: oracle_nft must be exactly 28 bytes (BLAKE2b-224 width — same as Cardano script/NFT policy id)
vectors.comment: 27-byte oracle_nft — Δ36 / VR-006 closure. A typo'd oracle_nft would silently never match any on-chain UTxO at validation time (fail-closed) — this manifest entry catches the divergence at sign time so the user is not stuck with an unclaimable policy. Pinning closes the asymmetry between the on-chain `is_valid_*_length` helpers and the off-chain encoder shape checks.

vectors.name: pool_script_hash_wrong_length
vectors.comment: 27-byte pool_script_hash — Δ36 / VR-006 closure. The pool validator hash is a BLAKE2b-224 output (28 bytes); a 27-byte hash would silently never resolve to the canonical pool address and the next ClaimWithAuth would fail post-mint.

vectors.name: pool_nft_wrong_length
vectors.rule: pool_nft must be exactly 28 bytes (Aegis canonical pool NFT policy id width)
vectors.comment: 27-byte pool_nft — Δ36 / VR-006 closure. The Aegis canonical pool NFT policy id is a 28-byte hash; the on-chain validator's `find_canonical_pool_output` matches on the policy id bytewise, so a wrong-length pool_nft silently never resolves. Encoded length must equal the Cardano hash width.

vectors.name: domain_tag_zero_length
vectors.rule: domain_tag must be exactly 27 bytes — the empty bytestring is rejected (signer_pkh consistency analogue)
vectors.comment: Empty domain_tag — Δ36 / VR-006 closure. The verification red-team's VR-006 also asked for a 'signer_pkh consistency' check; on-chain that check is enforced by `blake2b_224(awd.insured_vkey) == datum.insured` in ClaimWithAuth + RotateAuth (cannot be expressed as a single off-chain payload-shape rule because `insured_vkey` lives in the surrounding AuthWitnessDatum, not in AuthCoveragePayload). For symmetry we pin a different length-class boundary instead — a zero-length domain_tag — so the manifest exercises BOTH the boundary case (vector #1, 26 bytes) AND the empty case (this vector). Together they trap the full length-check surface.

vectors.name: max_coverage_negative
vectors.rule: max_coverage must be in [0, 2^63 - 1] — negative values rejected (signature-length analogue via int-range cross-stack pin)
vectors.comment: Negative max_coverage — Δ36 / VR-006 closure. The verification red-team's VR-006 asked for a '64-byte signature length' check; the AuthCoveragePayload schema does NOT carry the Ed25519 signature (it lives separately in the AuthWitnessDatum + ClaimWithAuth redeemer), so it cannot be exercised via a payload-shape vector. We pin the equivalent uint-range cross-stack symmetry instead: a negative integer field. Both stacks reject negatives via `assertNonNegInt`/`_assert_non_neg_int` — the int-range invariant is the load-bearing primitive for the signature-length check (once a non-negative bound is enforced, the off-chain wrappers around encode_auth_coverage_payload can stack the 64-byte signature length on top via their own assertions, both stacks already do this in `signAuthCommitment` / `sign_auth.py`).
